coredata.dc:title: Performance boosting of oxygen-deficiency WO 3-x quantum dots UV sensor by adding phosphoric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Hongxi
Wang, Ting
Tan, Mengdi
Du, Juan
Ma, Rong
Ren, Lijun
Feng, Haitao
Hu, Dengwei
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional inorganic quantum dots have rapidly developed in recent years due to their excellent properties. However, the application areas of two-dimensional inorganic quantum dots should be significantly expanded. UV light is extremely damaging to humans, especially the skin. At present, UV sensors are expensive and have a long fabrication process. In this work, the WO 3-x QDs were successfully synthesized by a simple solvothermal reduction method, which have abundant oxygen vacancies and an outstanding photochromic property. The photochromic performance of the WO 3-x QDs can be greatly improved by the addition of H3PO4. When the accumulated UV dosages of UVA, UVB, and UVC are greater than 150 J/m2, 50 J/m2 and 200 J/m2, respectively, the WO 3-x QDs/PA exhibits color changes.These results reveal the WO 3-x QDs/PA has good high sensitivity and good selectivity for UV lights. Furthermore, the color‑changing UV wearable was fabricated, and different time points were choose to detect UV light, the color changes of the UV wearable are significantly difference, which demonstrate the UV wearable has a broad application value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
WO 3-x quantum dots
Phosphoric acid
Optical materials and properties
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the UV sensor preparation process; (b) TEM images of WO 3-x QDs (inset: HRTEM image). (c) XRD pattern of WO 3-x QDs; (d) High-resolution W 4f spectrum of WO 3-x QDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the samples at 365 nm UV irradiation for 200 s. (b) ESR spectra of the samples before and after UV irradiation; (c) UV–vis spectra of WO 3-x QDs/PA under different exposure conditions (d) Absorbance at 624 nm as a function of UV dosages of WO 3-x QDs/PA. (e) Photos of WO 3-x QDs/PA solution under different dosages of UV lights.
(a) Schematic diagram of the UV wearable; (b) The color changes of the UV wearable at different time points under 10 s of sunlight.

head.ce:title: Performance boosting of oxygen-deficiency WO 3-x quantum dots UV sensor by adding phosphoric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Titanium Based Functional Materials and Devices in Universities of Shaanxi Province, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Baoji University of Arts and Sciences, Baoji 721013 Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Titanium Based Functional Materials and Devices in Universities of Shaanxi Province
Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Baoji University of Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Titanium Based Functional Materials and Devices in Universities of Shaanxi Province, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Baoji University of Arts and Sciences, Baoji 721013 Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional inorganic quantum dots have rapidly developed in recent years due to their excellent properties. However, the application areas of two-dimensional inorganic quantum dots should be significantly expanded. UV light is extremely damaging to humans, especially the skin. At present, UV sensors are expensive and have a long fabrication process. In this work, the WO 3-x QDs were successfully synthesized by a simple solvothermal reduction method, which have abundant oxygen vacancies and an outstanding photochromic property. The photochromic performance of the WO 3-x QDs can be greatly improved by the addition of H3PO4. When the accumulated UV dosages of UVA, UVB, and UVC are greater than 150 J/m2, 50 J/m2 and 200 J/m2, respectively, the WO 3-x QDs/PA exhibits color changes.These results reveal the WO 3-x QDs/PA has good high sensitivity and good selectivity for UV lights.

Furthermore, the color‑changing UV wearable was fabricated, and different time points were choose to detect UV light, the color changes of the UV wearable are significantly difference, which demonstrate the UV wearable has a broad application value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum dots (QDs) which show unique electronic structures have high application value in optoelectronic devices, biosensing, medical diagnosis, and treatment [1–4]. Recently, two-dimensional inorganic quantum dots have attracted the attention of researchers [5,6]. Among them, WO3-
x QDs have been reported with excellent fluorescence, electrochromic and photochromic properties due to the quantum confinement. In particular, the electrochromic and photochromic properties of WO3-
x QDs are better than other tungsten oxide-based nanomaterials [7,8]. Therefore, it would be of great interest to develop related chromic applications for WO3-
x QDs.

UV light is a double-edged sword with both pros and cons, which has been used for sterilization, health care, and the production of vitamin D by the body, while exposure to excessive UV light or chronic UV irradiation can lead to damage to the dermal tissue, such as pigment deposition, cutaneous aging, solar dermatitis, and skin cancer. Recently, many portable and wearable UV sensors have been developed based on optical and electrical measurements [9]. Compared to electronic UV wearables, color‑changing UV wearables are simple, inexpensive, portable, and convenient for obtaining the scale-up produce [10]. Color‑changing UV wearables mainly utilize the photochromic property of materials to realize UV detection. Among photochromic materials, inorganic oxide nanomaterials show the following advantages: long duration, high thermal stability, good fatigue resistance, and easy to shape [11]. However, the sensitivity and repeatability of color‑changing UV wearables based on inorganic oxide nanomaterials still needed to improve.

In this study, WO3-
x QDs were successfully synthesized using the polyol reduction method, and the photochromic property of WO3-
x QDs was evaluated. To further improve photochromic performance, phosphoric acid was considered as the additive to WO3-
x QDs, and the UV detection capabilities were analyzed. The color‑changing UV wearable was fabricated, and the value of practical application was also explored (Fig. 1 a).

The whole experimental process is briefly described below. First, WO3-
x QDs were synthesized according to the solvothermal method. 5.0 g of ammonium metatungstate hydrate was dissovled in 30 mL of ethylene glycol with continuous stirring for 30 min. After complete dissolution, the solution was transferred to a 50 mLTeflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Then, the yellow WO3-
x QDs solution was obtained. Second, different concentrations of phosphoric acid solutions were added to the WO3-
x QDs solution (2 mL). The UV wearables were manufactured using a sealing device on a band. The photochromic properties and other characterizations of the samples were performed by UV–vis absorption spectra, TEM, XRD, XPS, Raman spectrum, and EPR spectra. The additional experimental details are displayed in the Supplementary information.

Characterizations of the WO3-
x QDs are shown in Fig. 1b–d. Due to the presence of ethylene glycol, WO3-
x QDs occur with some degree of aggregation, and the size of WO3-
x QDs is approximately 15.2 nm (Fig. 1b) [12]. The interplanar spacing of 0.166 nm is found in the HRTEM image of WO3-
x QDs, which can be indexed as the (010) lattice plane (Fig. 2 c) [13]. The XRD pattern displays two broad diffraction peaks, and could be assigned to monoclinic WO2.72 (JCPDS no. 73–2177). The high-resolution W 4f spectrum of WO3-
x QDs shows two doublet peaks (W6+ and W5+) after deconvolution, the peaks of W5+ are located at 34.4 eV and 36.5 eV, indicating the presence of an oxygen vacancy defect in WO3-
x QDs. The high content of W5+ is determined from Fig. 1d (14.58 %).

The photochromic performance of the WO 3-x QDs and WO 3-x QDs/PA solutions was evaluated under the same conditions, as shown in Fig. 2a. After irradiation under UV light at 365 nm for 200 s (0.5 mW∙cm−2), all solutions change from colorless to blue, the WO 3-x QDs only appears with a wide absorption peak centered at 774 nm, while the WO 3-x QDs/PA displays two higher absorption peaks at 960 nm and 624 nm. The results indicate that photochromic performance of the WO 3-x QDs is significantly improved after PA addtion. The photochromic mechanisms of WO 3-x QDs and WO 3-x QDs/PA solutions are demonstrated by the ESR spectrum (Fig. 2b). Before UV irradiation, WO 3-x QDs and WO 3-x QDs/PA solutions only show an ESR signal at g = 2.01, which can be attributed to the oxygen vacancy defect in WO3-
x QDs, further demonstrating the presence of oxygen vacancies. The WO 3-x QDs/PA displays a stronger ESR signal than the WO 3-x QDs, which indicates the existence of more oxygen vacancies. After UV irradiation, the ESR signal at g = 2.01 intensity decreases, and two new ESR signals are discovered at g = 1.93 and g = 1.78. The new ESR signals can be assigned to the increased concentration of W5+, similar to W5+ in tungsten oxide heteronanoparticles [14] and reduced polyoxometalate [15]. The new ESR signals of WO 3-x QDs/PA are stronger than WO 3-x QDs, probably due to the addition of PA, some polyoxometalate-like clusters are formed in the solution. When the solutions are heated to 60 °C for 30 min, the ESR spectrum is restored to the initial condition. The amount of phosphoric acid was optimized to achieve the excellent photochromic performance of WO 3-x QDs/PA, the final concentration of PA is determined to be 10 mM (Fig. S4).

UV light at 365 nm, at 310 nm and at 254 nm are used to represent UVA, UVB and UVC light. The photochromic property of WO 3-x QDs/PA is evaluated under different light conditions (Fig. 2c). Visible light and near-infrared light cannot induce the color change of WO 3-x QDs/PA, while two absorption peaks appear in the spectrum under UVA, UVB and UVC light, indicating that the WO 3-x QDs/PA possess the excellent selectivity toward UV light. Furthermore, the WO 3-x QDs/PA was used as a UV light sensor, as shown in Fig. 2d and e. The intensity of absorption peak at 624 nm gradually increases with an increase in the accumulated UV doses, and UV–vis absorption spectra are also displayed in Fig. S4. Compared to UVC, the WO 3-x QDs/PA indicates a higher sensitivity to UVA and UVB. UVA and UVB are the primary cause of skin photodamage in sunlight, while UVC is absorbed by the ozone layer in the atmosphere and cannot reach the Earth’s surface. When the accumulated UV dosages of different UV lights are lower than 300 J/m2, the WO 3-x QDs/PA show good sensitivity to UVA, UVB, and UVC. In the beginning, no color change occurs for UVA, UVB, and UVC. The accumulated UV dosage is 50 J/m2, and the WO 3-x QDs/PA displays pale blue under UVB. When further increasing UV dosage to 150 J/m2, the WO 3-x QDs/PA starts to show color under UVA, however, UVC causes a color variation of WO 3-x QDs/PA greater than 200 J/m2.

The color changing UV wearable was manufactured based on the WO 3-x QDs/PA (Fig. 3 a), and was used to detect UV light in sunlight. On 8th August 2023, the accumulated UV dosage was characterized by the UV wearable. Five time points were selected: 10:00, 12:00, 14:00, 16:00, and 18:00 for the study (Fig. 3b), and the color changes of the UV wearable are significantly difference under solar light irradiation of 10 s. The color change at 14:00 is the maximum, indicating that the UV intensity is the highest and most damaging to the skin. The results show that the UV wearable has a broad application value in practical application.

In summary, we have successfully prepared WO3-
x QDs using a solvothermal reduction method. WO3-
x QDs were characterized by a series of characterization techniques, and reveal there are abundant oxygen vacancies in WO3-
x QDs. Furthermore, the photochromic performance of WO3-
x QDs is significantly boosted due to the addition of H3PO4, the mechanism is elucidated by the EPR spectrum. The UV detection ability of the WO 3-x QDs/PA was evaluated, and exhibits high sensitivity and good selectivity for UVA, UVB, and UVC. Further, a color‑changing UV wearable was used to monitor the ultraviolet intensity of sunlight, which displays a wide application value in practical application.

Hongxi Gu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ting Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mengdi Tan: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Juan Du: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Lijun Ren: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Haitao Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Dengwei Hu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51902004), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2019JLM-48), Technological Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2022TD-36), Shaanxi Provincial Education Department Foundation (No. 23JP003), and the Doctoral Scientific Research Starting Foundation of Baoji University of Arts and Sciences (No. ZK209010843)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136451.
